title: Rabbit litter tray and enclosure hygiene
subtitle: Safe litter types, cleaning frequency, and caecotroph management.
excerpt: Good enclosure hygiene prevents ammonia buildup, respiratory disease, and flystrike. Choosing the right litter and cleaning on the right schedule is straightforward once you know what to avoid.
sourceNotes: House Rabbit Society litter guide. RWAF housing and hygiene guidance. RSPCA rabbit welfare notes. Veterinary guidance on ammonia toxicity in small mammals.

glossaryTerms.definition: A soft, grape-like cluster of partially digested matter produced by the rabbit's caecum. Also called night droppings or soft pellets. Rabbits eat caecotrophs directly from the anus, usually at night. This is normal and essential for nutrition. Uneaten caecotrophs on the litter indicate a health problem, not poor hygiene.

A build-up of ammonia gas from urine decomposing in the litter or bedding. Even at low concentrations, ammonia irritates the rabbit's delicate nasal passages and can trigger or worsen respiratory infections. If you can smell it, levels are already too high.

A condition in which blowflies lay eggs in soiled or damp fur, particularly around the hindquarters. The hatching maggots feed on the rabbit's flesh. Flystrike can kill within hours. It is most common in warm weather and in rabbits with dirty living conditions or who cannot groom themselves properly.

content.text: Rabbits are naturally clean animals and will often choose one corner of their enclosure as a toilet area. Providing a litter tray in that corner makes cleaning easier and gives you a daily measure of the rabbit's health via its droppings.
Cleaning frequency
The litter tray should be emptied and refreshed daily. If the tray smells of
ammonia buildup
when you open the hutch door, cleaning frequency is not enough or the enclosure is not adequately ventilated.

Full enclosure cleans, including washing the tray with a pet-safe disinfectant and replacing all bedding, should be done once a week. More often in warm weather, when ammonia and bacteria build up faster.
In summer, check the hindquarters of each rabbit daily for soiling or signs of
flystrike
. Wet or matted fur around the tail or vent area must be cleaned and dried promptly. Any sign of maggots is a vet emergency.
Safe litter types
Not all litter materials are safe for rabbits. The following are suitable:
Paper-based pellet litter. Highly absorbent, dust-free, and safe if ingested in small amounts. The most widely recommended option.
Unscented compressed newspaper pellets. Similar properties to paper litter.
Meadow hay placed over a base of paper litter. Rabbits can forage while sitting in the tray, which encourages use. Top up hay daily.
Avoid the following:
Clumping clay litter. It forms a hard mass when wet and can cause a fatal blockage if ingested. Rabbits eat from their trays during the day.
Pine or cedar shavings. The aromatic oils (phenols) are toxic to rabbits and damage the liver and lungs with prolonged exposure. Kiln-dried pine shavings are less harmful but still not recommended.
Cat litter with silica crystals. Sharp and easily inhaled.
Corn cob litter. Can cause intestinal blockages if eaten.
Ammonia buildup and respiratory risk

Ammonia from urine is one of the main causes of respiratory disease in kept rabbits. Rabbits breathe only through their nose. Even low ammonia levels irritate the nasal passages over time. This leads to snuffles and bacterial infection.

Ventilation matters as much as cleaning frequency. A fully enclosed hutch with only a wire front panel traps ammonia-laden air. Ensure air can move through the sleeping area. In winter, balance ventilation with draught-proofing: moving air without direct wind.
Caecotroph management
A
caecotroph

is a soft, dark cluster of droppings made in the caecum. The rabbit eats it directly, usually overnight. This is normal and essential for digestion. It provides B vitamins and proteins that were not absorbed the first time through.

Finding uneaten caecotrophs in the tray or stuck to the rabbit's fur is not a hygiene problem. It is a health signal. The most common causes are obesity (the rabbit cannot reach its hindquarters), too much rich food (excess pellets suppress the urge to eat them), or dental or gut problems. Reduce pellets first. If uneaten caecotrophs persist after a week, see a vet.

Do not confuse caecotrophs with diarrhoea. Caecotrophs are soft but formed, clustered, and dark. Diarrhoea is watery, unformed, and often covers a wide area. True diarrhoea in a rabbit needs a vet the same day.
